← back

2023-12-10

Žižek on Israel-Palestine

[This is a corrected transcript of this interview of Slavoj Žižek by PoliticsJoe. There are 3 or 4 missing references, I ask for your help to provide them.]



- Slavoj Žižek, welcome back to PoliticsJoe, for those unfamiliar with who you are, how would you like to introduce yourself?



- Ah, now comes a surprise. I still advocate [for] communism, but what kind of communism? A German journalist asked me what kind of communist you are, and I gave an answer - a moderately conservative communist. By moderately conservative I mean something very precise. You know what bothers me with some leftist liberals- they make big plan[s], let’s do this, new legislation, and so on, but not the Rishi Sunak kind, but the authentic (they’re an endangered species today) old conservatives, what even Lenin and Mao admired in them, is this brutal realism. In the sense of, yes, we can do this, but you think like crazy about what will be the consequences- what if we screw that other thing and so on. And so because it isn’t the standard, even radical, leftist game. Then you of course cause a catastrophe, and then you blame the enemy. It’s like what I call, sadly in Africa, the Mugabe game. You screw it up and then you blame, because secretly the whites still control everything. And what is really breaking my heart is that now South Africa is approaching [this]. So by moderately conservative I mean simply, before you act, think well about all possible consequences because, social life here, I am very pessimist not traditional Marxist, is obscure, non-transparent, complex- you never know what the actual result would be. So now you can counterattack me, but why then communist at all? To give you a very short answer, it’s not a communism of joy, of abundance- it’s a sad pessimist communism. Look we are facing now, three, four, five, I cannot even nominate them all, real apocalyptic threats, war, immigration, ecological threat, artificial intelligence, and I don’t see any other way to cope with this than what I provocatively call war communism - an emergency state where, (I’m not talking about world government and [the] state taking over, that would be a catastrophe, even worse), but some kind of global international cooperation, not just this aesthetic cooperation- ‘yes we must understand each [other]’, but more forceful cooperation will be necessary. Now when people tell me, oh, but this is total Utopia, I tell them sorry, remember covid? Even Trump had to do measures which come very close to what I call communism. You remember uh every family got a check for $2,000 and so on, (stimulus checks) yeah and you remember how half a year before, if you were to mention an idea like this, all the neoliberals would say, no, this would mean [the] ruin of our economy. No, it didn’t. The good point about [the] Covid epidemic was that it was made clear how much we can play with the market, do things independently of the market and they don’t necessarily ruin the economy. So again, ‘communist’, it means I sincerely don’t see how to cope with the problems, threats, that are on the horizon, without a much stronger collective collaboration well beyond the limits of the sovereign nation-state. Just to conclude, please allow me this, my example that I mention all the time, you remember two and a half years ago, that crazy hot summer wherem northwest of United States, southwest of Canada, Seattle, Vancouver, 50° C, it was warmer than Mumbai or Emirates. You cannot say they should take care more of ecology, no these are rich parts of the world that did the proper measures. The problem is that the whole circulation of air around the North Pole is disturbed. So in this sense, I’m a communist in the sense of, we have to prepare to some emergency state. Precisely because if we don’t do it in a transparent democratic way,then better not to think [about] what can happen. But again moderately conservative, be careful about the consequences. I hate again this traditional leftist game was- yeah, yeah, imperialism still controls us, and so on.



- To continue the theme of catastrophe, you’ve also described the Western response to the conflict as a moral catastrophe. Could you talk about that a little bit more?

- The problem was [is] that now it’s getting more mixed. But this, you know, I was interrupted in my Frankfurt speech, because the basic problem was.. but I even didn’t use the word “but”, like I condemn Hamas “but”, and as somebody says at the end of the debate, the word “but” should be prohibited now. Another word that should be prohibited I was informed is “complex situation”, now there is only one side, [and] it’s absolutely clear. And you know where I see a moral catastrophe? Now, I’m sorry if I will sound anti-german, I don’t mean all of Germany, I mean the predominant stance in politics now’s this one - Germans admit they did the Holocaust. And now by pursuing radically pro-Israeli politics, they do something disgusting I think. They think that by supporting Israel where Israel is not right, West Bank and so on, they will somehow pay their debt for the Holocaust and so on. I find this something so terribly immoral. Another thing that I find immoral is also and at multiple levels, (and I here only quote Yuval Harari and all of them), this is for me the true moral catastrophe, where you posit a choice- either you speak about Israeli victims or you speak about Palestinian victims in Gaza. If you mention Palestinian victims, you are already anti-semitic and so on and so on. My my basic insight here is, that’s why I think it’s a moral catastrophe, that those zionist Israelis who mention in today’s situation Holocaust, are doing a terrifying disrespectful exploitation of Holocaust itself. Holocaust, a terrifying unimaginable crime, they use it as a stake as a tong to justify their politics now. That’s why I think that, and my true ethical hero, did you notice this detail, that’s why so many people attacked me, I was very restrained and careful in my Frankfurt speech. All the positive people I quote that were Jews! Even Moshe Dayan, how much more refreshing it’s now to read the first generation Ben-Gurion who didn’t play the game “this is our land blah blah blah”… No! they said it’s a[n] impossible conflict, we know nobody’s right and so on. Not to mention Simon Wiesenthal, not to mention my true ethical hero, if there is a person, to whom I would give, maybe (please to our listeners check it on Wikipedia or whatever), is Marek Edelman, it’s a polish jew mega hero. He was a bundist, more socialist jew, he was one of the key organizers of the ghetto uprising ’43 in Warsaw. Magically he survived it, then he was one of the key organizers of the Warsaw Uprising ’44. Magically he survived it then he remained in Poland and he gave a wonderful theoretical explanation, when later anti-semitism, under communism, emerged again, he stayed there. And he again, he provided a beautiful metaphor, he said, it’s not what I’m saying, but imagine me as a stone in Auschwitz, somebody has to be here to embody, not to talk about that memory. And at the end of his life, he wrote a letter to “my Palestinian friends”, where he said, I support your rebellion against Israel, of course, condemning all Palestinian violence. There is a great Jewish tradition which is incredible. That’s why I don’t like this Western, the most disgusting Western position, anti-semitic Zionist, of not too many Jews here but you go there and protect our civilization. No! I like Jews here also! Wherever they are they make a great contribution to our civilization and so on. Look precisely because they were stateless, we cannot even imagine European modernity, enlightenment, and so on without the jew. The very idea of enlightenment universalism came from the Jews who simply, like they were condemned to be universalist, you know. But you know where the, sorry just one thing, you know Netanyahu and others when you ask them why is that place Palestine yours? They said, look at the Bible, it tells everything, and so on. But in the most ironic case of cancel culture, mention the Bible, and you will be excluded as anti-semitic. Once I did this, in a debate in New York I think some jew said, but sorry we are attached to our tradition, look at the Bible. So I told them I quoted him I said yes let’s look at the Bible. You know Moses 40 years wandering around Sinai then up on the mountain, Moses dies he already sees the valley and then Joshua takes over, and what to do? And God’s voice tells him Canaan, Canaanites, are down there they are evil people kill them all, men, women, children and and so on. So you know if I were a Jew I wouldn’t insist too much on the Bible, you know. But to avoid a misunderstanding I’m not here condemning the Jews because at that time let’s be frank, everybody was acting like this. We Slavs from some Siberian steps I don’t know where, how did we come to Europe? By invading it and so on. So I’m just saying that in the same way that we talk about invented traditions, we should also treat our uh historical documents, and now I will make a very sad conclusion. I am very skeptical about this idea that originally religions are good. Islam, Judaism, Christianity, it’s only in their political application that they are misused. No, you find a lot of horror and cheat already in the original. So I’m here much more of a pessimist. The lesson of this, I immediately conclude sorry, the lesson of this is, we are left to our own resources, in Lacanian terms theoretical[ly] there is no big other, by big other I don’t mean just God but some global symbolic order which tells you what to do. No, we live in a very dangerous era where no reference to tradition or to science or whatever can tell us what to do.



- I’d also, in the tradition of kind of emancipatory Judaism encourage listeners to consult the Wikipedia of Dennis Goldberg who was imprisoned on Robin Island and actually separated, he was part of the anti-apartheid campaign in South Africa and also in prison there with Mandela, but of course, separated from him because of apartheid and so was alone in prison for the same period of time (Žižek - why because he was a Jew or what?) because Mandela was black and he was a Jew he was white so…



- Yeah because, I know, sorry to interrupt this, I know the situation there very well, you know even now in India many people, ‘many’ (laughs), some people, (many don’t know about me) hate me because I killed or at least undermined the respect for another sacred cow there, Gandhi. You know when Gandhi was in India, yes he fought against apartheid, but only for the Indian minority. Read Gandhi’s text, my friends republish them now, he doesn’t put in doubt the big separation black, white. He just says we Indians should be counted as white and so on. The second thing you see, this is what I mean [by] moderately conservative. Maybe you read it, I think I already used this somewhere, a couple of months ago at Birkbeck College where I’m temporarily employed, there was a debate with a lady from South Africa, hardline old ANC fighter, and I asked her a frank question. I asked her, in all our media I read all the time about how South Africa is on the border of becoming a failed… on the edge of becoming a failed state blah blah blah, I don’t want to believe Western media please tell me how do I reply to this? And I was almost crying, so sad, you know what she told me? She told me that more and more the predominant atmosphere, [and] feeling, in the poor black majority, is nostalgia for apartheid. She said that now in memory they remember apartheid as a time where yes they were second-class citizens, but if anything, the standard of living was the same maybe a little bit even higher for the poor black majority. Plus it was a police state which means there was safety. Now society is disintegrating, South Africa has one of the highest rape numbers per capita. You see this is what I want the left to avoid. How these eternal stories, look at Latin America, Bolivia, but now they did not do a wise thing, they cut diplomatic ties with Israel, but till now I believed in Bolivia, because I was friendly with Vice President Morales Álvaro García Linera and so on, they were 12 years in power without scaring too much the capital. They succeeded, the standard of living went up and then you remember there was a coup d’état against them, but they won again… So that’s my moderate conservatism, how to avoid, I will name just three names- Cuba, Venezuela, [and] Nicaragua, where it was a catastrophe, the left has to ask very hard questions here. I don’t want to be that comfortable leftist who organizes a revolution, a revolution fails and then you move back to your academic post and write a thick book [on] how because of the imperialist cunningness and plot it had to fail the revolution. No, the left deserves some authentic success.



- Can we interrogate a little bit more the role of violence in revolution in liberation struggle, whether it’s, as we were talking about the anti-apartheid campaign in South Africa, or well, take any any sort of struggle against injustice… the one we’ve been interrogating so far is obviously with Palestine and Israel, is there an acceptable level of violence to your mind in a liberation struggle?



- I’m not naive here. I know that in certain conditions, if nothing else, a certain level of violence paradoxically creates the conditions for a peaceful revolution. I forgot the guy’s name, I’m an old senile, but a guy, a black guy [reference needed] I think wrote a wonderful book I quote it, I forgot where in, so new text, where he says that look for example, at America in the 60s and so on, struggle against racism. Yes, Martin Luther King won, but it also won because it was clear to the white majority there if there is not a threat of violence behind, Luther King wouldn’t be taken seriously. Iit was the same in South Africa- yes, Mandela won but because the apartheid guys were well aware that there was a much more radical orientation within the ANC threatening with violence. The most tragic example here, let’s look into history, is Haiti, where I mean the exploitation of Haiti till the Haiti revolution around 1800 was breathtaking, let’s say that profits from Haiti brought to the French state, more than half of its income, but what I want to say is this- revolution won. The first generation Toussaint Louverture, were very fair towards the whites. They just freed slaves but their main worry was to keep the production going, [and] economic collapse to avoid[?]. Then the French, Napoleon is here the bad guy, he brought, arrested Toussaint Louverture, brought him to France and slowly killed him in an abandoned castle blah blah… Then the next generation were more radical, violent, you know what they did? they, I forgot his name, the successor of Toussaint Louverture (General Dessalines), he simply gave an order to kill all white people who remained there, with one exception, that’s true, Polish people. You know the story it’s one of the most beautiful revolutionary legend, it again makes me cry, when Napoleon sent an army to Haiti with a horrible order- kill all the blacks, he said, after the revolution it’s not just to enslave them again, kill them all, bring new slaves. Then when the French army was approaching the Haiti[an] black army revolutionaries, they heard some singing and they thought yeah this must be some primitive African dancing, when they come closer you know what they recognize? You know what the black freed slaves were singing? La Marseillaise, and thousands of Polish soldiers said wait a minute, are we here on the right side and they change sides, and so on. Napoleon won, but then the result of all this trauma was this terrifying order to kill all white people. Now you can say you see if you give them freedom what happens, ah! it’s more complex. Because, at precisely this moment the new black rulers took over [and] imitated perfectly the French aristocratic system, they named counts and so on. You know, this violence serves the establishment as a pure copy of European feudal aristocracy there, but Haiti nonetheless did something prohibited too… Do you know that to be reintegrated into [the] world economy Haiti had to pay to France, as for the damage of lost property, slaves, in throughout 19th century it was at some point up to 80% its state budget. You know when the last money was paid to France in 1947, I think, so it consciously they kept, but so I don’t avoid your question, I would put it like this, first my limit is if they’re not well known as a gangster family or what, don’t attack civilian population. Keep it to [the] army there, if the situation is really terrorist, I condone a limited amount of violence. But we should always be aware, again, of this ambiguity that you know you need a violent wink, so that, violence should be more used as a threat to do the necessary compromise. That would be my formula.



- So let’s interrogate the nature of modern conflict, it’s asymmetrical always, whether it’s the British and American imperial forces in Afghanistan, whether it’s the Palestinians against the Israelis.. but in this case, and to say that the Palestinians should confront the Israelis militarily, it’s essentially a death sentence, you can say, great, fantastic, morally justifiable, but completely ineffective as a tactic and it makes me uncomfortable because what I’m essentially saying, the place I’m left in is essentially justifying the attack on civilians which something morally ethically for me I find deeply distressing, deeply upsetting but I don’t see any other way in terms of an effective military campaign, an effective terrorist campaign the use of limited violence to achieve political ends. You know if they attack military bases they will all die, so what else can they do if not kill the civilians and I find that deeply deeply uncomfortable because like I said…



- Okay now I will accept this totally pragmatic ruthless thinking, but shouldn’t have then focus a little bit more on some effectively violent groups of settlers or whatever, and not precisely that part which was closest to them, in some sense. I mean here I agree with you let’s begin by this - the situation is tragic in some sense. You cannot propose a simple solution and so like this is the formula and so on, no I see your point of course, but again as you already pointed out, not only this means suicide for Palestinians, but in the long term such suicidal attacks eternalise war and I don’t see then any peaceful ANC, Mandela or whatever arising out of this, and bringing peace. That’s what horrifies me, you know. I would even accept attacksm, here we agree, if there is a hope that they will serve the way. The threat with violence was necessary to bring in Mandela, to bring in Martin Luther King, and so on. Otherwise I, what really as friend of the Jews and of Palestinians, I see really it scares me again, a new wave of anti-semitism. It will, I don’t trust zionists but I also see very clearly anti-semitism where there is one. And what I’mm again, what horrifies me is this identification of that anti-semitism as the result of this war will be elevated into one of the main markers of anti-colonial struggle. Although you know again I like to complicate things. The Jewish side is not clean here. You know I did something cunning, I read Theodor Herzl, founder of zionism, you know to whom, (good for his soul, he didn’t send it), but you know that he wrote a letter to Cecil Rhodes, asking him for advice, and would he invest money, addressing him in terms of colonisation, you colonised half of Africa help us to colonise it there. Second thing, he explicitly flirted, Herzlm already with the Western-right, using these terms of today’s right that the state of Israel, a Jewish State, there will be the impregnable wall to protect Western Europe from Oriental barbarism. So that’s the mega tragedy of Zionism, that what the greatest victims of European anti-semitism chose the way to oppress others there, and got caught into this way. Here, I hated Ahmadinejad, you know the ex-president of Iran, I even at some point was prohibited to enter because there were some elections 10 years ago where Ahmadinejad won by cheating and so on and I wrote some letters. But you know when he said something that others also said, okay Germans did something horrible which Ahmadinejad didn’t admit, but let’s admit it, Holocaust, isn’t there something cheating in giving them the land in Pal- wouldn’t it be normal for Germans, listen end of World War II you also have a lot of victims which means empty land, give, why don’t you, you who killed six million Jews, give him I don’t know the city of Hamburg, and you know there is some cheating in it. As if you know as if I borrow from you 1 billion dollars, and then instead of paying you back I say take that guy’s money. You know the situation is desperately complex. This is why this is not a pessimism. The starting point is there is no clean solution here. PolitcsJoe: And as you know as well as the word “but”, “complex situation” is a banned phrase in this conversation as you said at the beginning… especially by this now rediscovered, is totally pro-israeli pol- yeah they, you know an article in I forgot which German daily newspaper, attacking me said, they put it as a title of their text “Hamas has no context”. Like it’s madness! like you know every mention of context is… but then I answered in, I think quite that even if we… it’s more complex, just condemn Hamas, of course, we should inquire into context because for example, there are dozens, hundreds of books of the origins of Nazism. And it’s clear that there was a crisis in Germany experienced by millions of people economic crisis and so on, and Hitler profited from that crisis offering a certain anti-semitic narrative and to many ordinary people things were all of a sudden clear. You know like ah now we know, behind all of these are the jews, and so on. So here is a nice example of how contextualising doesn’t mean justifying in any way. Evil can be radical crazy evil but it’s always a product of certain circumstances. It exploded in certain circumstances like the same again in Haiti the second generation who killed all the white people, yes I agree it was horrible but let’s take a look at how breathtaking the exploitation in Haiti was. Okay I will not go on, I talk too much.



- No, not at all, not at all, I think there’s one other sort of area I’d like to explore on Israel-Palestine before we maybe move on. So between the two extremes the two totalitarian positions that we’ve discussed the one of Hamas and the one of Ben-Gvir and Smotrich that sort of hard, hard right Zionist position but it’s not just only Zionist, this sort of greater Israel idea, the expulsion of the Palestinians from Gaza, and if you look at demographic changes happening in Israel the younger generation skew much more to that extreme right-wing politics, they skew much more towards the politics of the likes of Ben-Gvir and Smotrich and that surely means looking to the future whether there’s a solution whether, without sounding like Tony Blair, a third way somewhere in between those two total extreme poles, because the Israeli population electorally is going to move towards that extreme, so if anything the prospect of peace and a lasting peace, seems further away than perhaps it ever has been…



- First I would say something that may surprise you. I read a good analysis, leftist analysis, of the situation in France and Belgium, and they discovered the same thing there, among these, I don’t like the terms, “pro-terrorist” Arab immigrants. So it’s not that they, we were not open enough to integrate them, no, most of this young generation of terrorists who did that concert hall bombing (Bataclan) remember a couple of years ago in Paris, do you know that there they are second generation? It’s the same paradox that the older generation, their parents were much better integrated into French society. So the second thing one has to mention, I’m sorry if it will sound racist, is that a lot of this younger generation are East European jews who are from Russia and other East European countries who tend to be much more aggressive, racist than the first generation. There really is something very dark, this is the true dark side of the East European till ’89 communism. For example, maybe you know the story I spoke with a German dissident I don’t like him too much but okay he was well known Waltz Beerman[reference needed] singer who immigrated to the West, friend of Brecht and so on, and he told me that in early 90s immediately after the reunification he went to some big ecological meeting in ex-East Germany and there was there a strong group of, well to call them by their name, Neo-Nazi eco-people, ecologically, and he told them, but you are for Hitler, you know what answer he got? He told me one of the most terrifying answers that I got, no, we criticise Hitler because Hitler did some good things like getting rid of the Jews but also he developed car industry he built highways and so on which was horrible you know this most obscene version so… This is what always surprised me that even now, even now, the right wing Neo-Nazi parties are much stronger in ex-East Germany than in the west. This is what again really makes me a pessimist, that what you described as something going on among the Israeli youth is also going on among the Palestinians up to a point… even in the United States look [at] all of these Proud Boys and so on and so on, and what really makes me afraid is that liberal democracy the way we knew it, is coming to an end. Let’s for a moment forget about West Bank, Israel let’s move to the United States. I think United States it’s really in danger on the edge of the civil war. I forget his name because he’s a disgusting nobody, who is now the new house speaker, it’s not McCarthy, it’s the Trump new one young one, he’s like the Trumpist (Mike Johnson) but the ultra Trump… after people like him and DeSantis we will even have, I predict, a nostalgia for Trump because Trump was very careful you know not to pass a certain limit, because this guy openly advocates violating the constitution, problematising democracy. People notice very nicely that he never refers to the United States as democracy, he always uses the term republic. Republic in this Christian fundamentalist sense and so on and so on. So I don’t have a good counterpoint to you, my counterpoint is much more sad is that the whole world is moving in this direction. Look at the catastrophe barely avoided, if it is avoided, remember a couple of weeks ago the first round of the presidential elections in Argentina, where I again, I prefer to erase the names of really bad people, some right-wing liberal populist, again he went to the end, a nightmare, and I always use this when people ask me about freedom, you know since I also published by Bloomsbury a book on freedom (Freedom, a disease without cure), and I try not to relativize the term of freedom but to see many dimensions contradictory he radicalizes this guy, something with M, the notion of freedom. Did you read, I couldn’t believe my eyes when I read it, I had, you know I like this scene from old cartoons Tom and Jerry when you see something and your eyes jump out and turn the air, you know what he advocates? He said this is a new freedom to solve people from poverty, to give to poor people the right to sell their organs and their children. Sorry, this is liberal capitalism today. And so that’s the cause of my pessimism. Yesterday at a debate on that strange place, I liked it, Royal Institute, you know somebody attacked me along these lines with a very good question no, somebody told me, I like but your messages we are **ed up, no trouble, but you in any case you are old you will soon die, so you don’t care, what about the majority of young people here? Can you/ shouldn’t you give them some hope? In my counterpoint was that his question is very ambiguous. Does he mean that that it’s true that we are [ __ ] up just out of respect for innocent and young people we should give them some hope? I totally oppose this this means patronising them. Or does he mean there is hope, your analysis is wrong. And I would like to believe I am, that there is some hope, my motto is the one from old Frankfurt School, Max Horkheimer says this, a propo of Nazism, that our stance today should be pessimism in theory, optimism in practice. And I try to follow it, I’m generally a pessimist but whenever there is a chance, whatever to do I was engaged against Putin for Pussy Riot and so on, I’m in contact with this may surprise you, with some Guerilla group in Philippines and so on, whereverm I quite liked Aristide, Jean-Bertrand Aristide in Haiti, he was dirtily manipulated by the United States, he was not a crazy Castro like utopian, he was very modest, honest but it was too much, so for a long time I supported Syriza and I still am not sure could they…my reproach to them is again moderately conservative because they complained how the European Union treated them, but what did they expect? you know yeah like I think that absolutely use any… in India there I’m for Muslims engaged in things against the horror that Modi is doing now. Modi is playing the same game as Trump and the new right, some people protested, non-hindus, that they don’t feel safe you know what was Mod’s answer? public, Mod’s answer was yeah and it’s good so, so that you will know that you are the threat of the nation, and so on. It’s literal falling apart of this basic safety of the public order. Now in my old age this may sound horrible to you but the right is today the one which is more and more populist, violent, and so on. And I think now I will say something shocking to you, it’s an old story but maybe I hope you are not an idiot, not to you but for others… maybe the left should begin to use the term moral majority, look many people I don’t think there were most anti-semites who demonstrated here hundreds of thousand and there are for Palestine and there are probably many more who are afraid to, my friends, Israeli friends, are now arrested in Israel and so on, we should address them we should maybe drop this violence, public violence, speech and, no, my image of the United States is this one with a little bit of irony but I think it’s true. Look, my old story when on the 6th of January the Trump mob occupied the Congress, my leftist friends were crying, we should have been doing it this is our, I told them no, sorry you live in the wrong time. Today the game the rules of the game have changed. If we mean by postmodernism, this cynical, relativism, flirting with violence, blah blah then if there ever was a postmodern president, historicist, relativist, it’s Donald Trump and let’s face it if there ever was a person who embodies [it] at its best the this moral majority simple in a progressive sense let’s say conservative in the sense of there should be basic decency attitude is (indeed?) Bernie Sanders, I think we should not leave all these voters to the new right. I didn’t meet Sanders himself, but I spoke with his ghost writer with some people around him who told me Bernie knows this, he, Bernie said when there were elections, Trump versus Hillary he said something ingenious he said, don’t worry about because Democrats under Hillary fell into this trap. They worried about the middle, will w, e if we come to radical alienate the this undecided middle classes. Bernie said no, we should aim at Trump’s voters… many of them are disillusioned poor whites and so on, they should be our target that would be if you ask me my politics today.



The rest of the interview goes on to discuss culture and movies, with an insight to Oppenheimer and Bhagavat Gita, check the the interview from 58:06.







submit missing resources:
